notes.anchor: T. Manlius, who carries on business at Thespiae
notes.note: Thespiae was a Boeotian city (central Greece) notable for its cult of Eros and proximity to Helicon; it lay outside Achaea proper but within Servius's sphere of influence as proconsul. T. Manlius is otherwise unknown; the phrase negotiatur Thespiis identifies him as a Roman businessman (negotiator) resident in Greece.

notes.anchor: he is no stranger to our pursuits
notes.note: "Our pursuits" (studia nostra) is Cicero's habitual phrase for literary and philosophical culture; saying a businessman shares these interests is high praise in Ciceronian terms, implying he is not merely a money-man but an educated Roman gentleman.

notes.anchor: Varro Murena is keenly anxious to do everything for his sake
notes.note: "Varro Murena" is A. Terentius Varro Murena, who had been adopted into the gens Licinia Murena; he appears in several of Cicero's 46--44 BC letters as an active friend and sometime advocate, and is likely the same man later consul suffect in 23 BC and executed for conspiracy against Augustus.

notes.anchor: if you assist and dignify T. Manlius as much as ever you can, in whatever ways you can do so honourably and consistently with your standing
notes.note: The phrase pro tua dignitate (consistently with your standing) is a standard epistolary safeguard: Cicero acknowledges the proconsul cannot do anything improper, but presses him to do as much as his office permits --- a polite framing of a substantial request for official favour.
